Preface
This is a Quarto book.

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: 1  Recap on Linear Models
text:
1.1 Packages
We will be using the following packages in this section:
library(dplyr)  # for data wrangling
library(readr)  # for importing data
library(ggplot2)  # for plotting

title: 1  Recap on Linear Models
text:
1.2 Building up the Linear Model
A common behavioural task in language and word recognition research is the Lexical Decision Task. In Lexical Decision Tasks, we present real words (e.g., table) and word-like pseudowords (e.g., frimp). We then measure people’s responses and their response times to each item.
The best predictor of response time to real words is word frequency: response times are faster to words that occur more often.
Here is a plot of some made-up data (based on real estimates) showing a relationship between Zipf word frequency [a log-transformed count variable; van Heuven et al. (2014)] and response time (ms). Each point is depicts one word.









If you want to follow along, you can load the data into your R session with:

dat <- read_csv("https://jackedtaylor.github.io/AOMS5/sims_lm.csv")

How could we describe these data?

title: 1  Recap on Linear Models
text:
1.3 The Average (Intercept)
One very simple but powerful description could be to just calculate the average log response time.

mean(dat$log_rt)

[1] 6.397823


Typically, this is termed the Intercept, and is written as \(\beta_0\).
If we were to try to predict our dependent variable using just the intercept, a standard way of writing this would be:
\[y_i = \beta_0\]
We could plot our prediction as a red line on our plot from earlier:

ggplot(dat, aes(zipf, log_rt)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = mean(dat$log_rt),
    colour = "red"
  )








This is a start, but it ignores the clear effect of frequency (Zipf) on our response times.

title: 1  Recap on Linear Models
text:
1.4 Intercept + Slope
In addition to the intercept, we can calculate a slope. This is usually written as \(b_1\). It is multiplied by our predictor, and represents the change in \(y_i\) associated with a one-unit increase in \(x\) for item \(i\).
\[ logRT_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 zipf_i \]
Whereas before we could just calculate the mean, now we need to estimate these parameters. R has a handy lm() function for this.

lm(log_rt ~ 1 + zipf, data = dat)


Call:
lm(formula = log_rt ~ 1 + zipf, data = dat)

Coefficients:
(Intercept)         zipf  
    6.85171     -0.09913  


We could plot this like so:

ggplot(dat, aes(zipf, log_rt)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(intercept=6.85171, slope=-0.09913, colour="red")














Note



Note that the model intercept \(\beta_0\) is now 6.851709, rather than 6.3978225. This is because the intercept actually specifies the value of our red line when \(x_i=0\). If we want \(\beta_0\) to still reflect the mean, we can just mean-centre zipf.

dat <- mutate(dat, zipf_cent = zipf - mean(zipf))

ggplot(dat, aes(zipf_cent, log_rt)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(intercept=6.398, slope=-0.09913, colour="red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept=0, linetype="dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept=6.398, linetype="dashed")










One last issue is that our model is only predicting values along the red line. Yet none of our observations fall perfectly on the line. There is some variability, or “noise”, around our estimates.

title: 1  Recap on Linear Models
text:
1.5 Intercept + Slope + Residual
“Residual” is a term often used to refer to unexplained variability. We could calculate it as everything left unexplained after we have accounted for the intercept.

dat <- mutate(
  dat,
  pred = 6.85171 + -0.09913 * zipf,
  e_i = pred - log_rt
)

We could visualise these residuals as the distance from the line:

ggplot(dat, aes(zipf, log_rt)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(intercept=6.85171, slope=-0.09913, colour="red") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = zipf, yend = pred))








A powerful way of capturing such variability around estimates is as a normal distribution. If we plot the residuals we can see that they are indeed roughly normal:

ggplot(dat, aes(e_i)) +
  # plot the density of e_i
  geom_density() +
  # plot a normal distribution for reference
  geom_function(
    fun = dnorm, colour = "red",
    args = list(mean=0, sd=sd(dat$e_i))
  ) +
  xlim(-0.5, 0.5)








This noise is typically written as \(e_i\). We specify it as being drawn from a normal distribution with a mean of zero and a standard deviation of \(\sigma\):
\[\begin{aligned}
logRT_i &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 zipf_i + e_i \\
e_i &\sim N(0, \sigma)
\end{aligned}\]
We now have 3 free parameters that are estimated by the lm() function in R:

\(b_0\): (Intercept)
\(b_1\): zipf
\(\sigma\): Residual standard error


summary( lm(log_rt ~ 1 + zipf, data = dat) )


Call:
lm(formula = log_rt ~ 1 + zipf, data = dat)

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-0.245604 -0.078863 -0.000974  0.082422  0.267120 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  6.851709   0.045030  152.16  < 2e-16 ***
zipf        -0.099130   0.009051  -10.95 1.18e-14 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.1246 on 48 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7142,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7083 
F-statistic:   120 on 1 and 48 DF,  p-value: 1.184e-14

title: 1  Recap on Linear Models
text:
1.6 Simulating New Data
Now that we have estimated those parameters, we can simulate a new dataset using the formula.
Here is the formula we will be using:
\[\begin{aligned}
logRT_i &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 Zipf_i + e_i \\
e_i &\sim N(0, \sigma)
\end{aligned}\]
First, we will define the parameters used in the simulation:

# intercept
b0 <- 6.85171

# slope for the effect of Zipf
b1 <- -0.09913

# standard deviation of the residuals
s <- 0.1246

# number of observations to simulate
n <- 50

Then, we can plug these in to simulate log response times for a simulated sample of words:

# build a simulated dataset
sim_dat <- tibble(
  # random Zipf values from 1.5 to 7.5
  zipf = runif(n, 1.5, 7.5),
  # simulate residuals as Normal(0, sigma)
  e_i = rnorm(n, 0, s),
  # simulate log response times using the formula above
  log_rt = b0 + b1 * zipf + e_i
)

Finally, we can plot this, to show how the simulated dataset shows a very similar pattern to the original data:

ggplot(sim_dat, aes(zipf, log_rt)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", colour="red")












van Heuven, Walter J. B., Pawel Mandera, Emmanuel Keuleers, and Marc Brysbaert. 2014. “SUBTLEX-UK: A New and Improved Word Frequency Database for British English.” Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology 67 (6): 1176–90. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2013.850521.

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.1 Why Linear Mixed-Effects Models?
In the section recapping linear models, we used a linear model to describe the relationship between word frequency and log response time in a lexical decision task. In these data we had one observation per word.
In a real study, however, we would have multiple observations for each word from each participant. The traditional approach would be to aggregate: calculate the average log response time for each word before fitting the model. However, while this allows us to account for between-item variability, by aggregating over participants, we are losing valuable information about how the data were generated.
Where it was possible, a common compromise could be to fit the model twice, and then examine whether a hypothesis is supported across both items and participants (Raaijmakers et al. 1999). However, a more robust solution would be to model item and participant variability simultaneously. This is something that linear-mixed effects models allow us to do (Locker et al. 2007).

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
section: 2.2 What is a Linear Mixed-Effects Model?
text:
2.2 What is a Linear Mixed-Effects Model?
A linear mixed-effects model is largely a generalisation of the ideas behind a linear model that we went over in the recap on linear models. A key difference is that rather than aggregating, we can model multiple sources of variability simultaneously.
To start with, we will just work with random intercepts:
\[logRT_{ip} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 Zipf_{ip} + I0_i + P0_p + e_{ip}\] Our formula is very similar to before, with the following key differences:

We index observations with two indices: \(i\) (item) and \(p\) (participant).
We include \(I0_i\): per-item random intercepts
We include \(P0_p\): per-participant random intercepts

As we saw with \(e_i\) in the linear model example, \(I0_i\), \(P0_p\), and \(e_{ip}\) are all drawn from normal distributions with their own standard deviations:
\[\begin{aligned}
I0_i &\sim N(0, \sigma_{I0}) \\
P0_p &\sim N(0, \sigma_{P0}) \\
e_{ip} &\sim N(0, \sigma_e)
\end{aligned}\]
The per-item and per-participant random intercepts describe variability among these populations. Items will vary in their average response time — while word frequency is maybe the best predictor of response times, a whole range of other factors will also affect a single word’s average response time. Similarly, participants will differ in their average response times, with some participants responding consistently faster, and some responding consistently slower. Our model says that these random effects combine with our fixed effects to generate our observations.
To get a sense for what this looks like, in this section we’ll use these formulae to simulate some data.

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.3 Packages
We’ll use the following packages in this section:

library(lme4)  # for fitting LMMs
library(dplyr)  # for data wrangling
library(tidyr)  # for data wrangling
library(readr)  # for importing data
library(ggplot2)  # for plotting

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.4 Defining Parameters
Here we define all the free parameters from the equations above:

\(\beta_0\) (Fixed-effect Intercept): b_0
\(\beta_1\) (Fixed-effect Slope): b_1
\(\sigma_{I0}\) (SD of per-item random intercepts): s_P0
\(\sigma_{P0}\) (SD of per-participant random intercepts): s_I0
\(\sigma_e\) (SD of the residual): s_e

The numbers here are based on the same real data as in the section on linear models.

# Fixed-effect intercept and slope
# (same as the parameters in the section on linear models)
b_0 <- 6.85
b_1 <- -0.1

# SD of per-item random intercepts
s_I0 <- 0.08

# SD of per-participant random intercepts
s_P0 <- 0.16

# residual SD
s_e <- 0.33

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.5 Simulating Items and Participants
To be able to simulate trials (a single participant responds to a single item), we first need to simulate the individual items and participants.
Here we build two dataframes, of 50 items and 30 participants.

# simulate 50 items
n_items <- 50

items <- tibble(
  # item IDs
  i_id = 1:n_items,
  # item word frequencies
  zipf = runif(n_items, 1.5, 7.5),
  # item intercepts
  I0_i = rnorm(n_items, 0, s_I0)
)

# simulate 30 participants
n_participants <- 30

participants <- tibble(
  # participant IDs
  p_id = 1:n_participants,
  # participant intercepts
  P0_p = rnorm(n_participants, 0, s_P0)
)

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.6 Visualising Random Intercepts
As you can see, the random intercepts allow us to describe multiple sources of variability at the same time. A common way of understanding random effects is to plot separate linear relationships for each unit in our population.
For example, just as we can plot a single simulated linear relationship, so too can we plot how this would look for items and participants in a given sample:
# (a)
ggplot(items) +
  geom_abline(aes(intercept = b_0+I0_i, slope=b_1, group=i_id)) +
  geom_abline(intercept=b_0, slope=b_1, colour="red") +
  xlim(1.5, 7.5) +
  ylim(4.5, 7.5)
# (b)
ggplot(participants) +
  geom_abline(aes(intercept = b_0+P0_p, slope=b_1, group=p_id)) +
  geom_abline(intercept=b_0, slope=b_1, colour="red") +
  xlim(1.5, 7.5) +
  ylim(4.5, 7.5)






(a) Per-Item Variability







(b) Per-Participant Variability





Here, the red line shows the fixed effect, while the black lines show variability between (a) items and (b) participants. You can clearly see that there is less variability between items (s_I0 = 0.08) than between participants (s_P0 = 0.16).

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.7 Simulating Trials
In a trial, a single participant responds to a single item. We simulate trials by simulating the meeting between participants and items.
As DeBruine and Barr (2021) do, we here simulate this using the crossing() function from tidyr. This simulates a study in which all 30 participants respond to 50 items, producing a total 1500 trials. For each unique combination of item and participant, we also simulate the residuals, \(e_{ip}\).

# generate trials
trials <- crossing(items, participants) |>
  mutate(e_ip = rnorm(n(), 0, s_e))

# print the first few rows
print(trials)

# A tibble: 1,500 × 6
    i_id  zipf   I0_i  p_id     P0_p    e_ip
   <int> <dbl>  <dbl> <int>    <dbl>   <dbl>
 1     1  6.80 0.0698     1 -0.00396  0.518 
 2     1  6.80 0.0698     2  0.192    0.0523
 3     1  6.80 0.0698     3  0.0264   0.147 
 4     1  6.80 0.0698     4  0.220    0.141 
 5     1  6.80 0.0698     5 -0.177   -0.642 
 6     1  6.80 0.0698     6 -0.0287   0.236 
 7     1  6.80 0.0698     7  0.152   -0.124 
 8     1  6.80 0.0698     8  0.00549  0.445 
 9     1  6.80 0.0698     9  0.0114  -0.218 
10     1  6.80 0.0698    10  0.115   -0.212 
# ℹ 1,490 more rows








Note



As well as simulating all possible combinations of items and participants, you could also simulate other combinations of participants and items. For example, you could simulate participants responding to N random trials, or you could create 10 separate stimulus lists, with 10% of your participants being assigned to each set of items. Similarly, you could use real data to inform your simulation, such as taking real items and real Zipf values from a frequency corpus.

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.8 Simulating Responses
Now that we have simulated trials, we can use our formula to simulate each participant’s response to each item.
As a quick reminder, here is the formula again:
\[logRT_{ip} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 Zipf_{ip} + I0_i + P0_p + e_{ip}\]
We use this formula to add the simulated log response times to the trials dataframe:

trials <- mutate(
    trials,
    log_rt = b_0 + b_1 * zipf + I0_i + P0_p + e_ip
  )

If we plot these observations coloured by items or participants, you can see how item and participant variability is reflected in the structure of the data:
# (a)
ggplot(trials, aes(zipf, log_rt, colour=factor(i_id))) +
  geom_point() +
  theme(legend.position="none")
# (b)
ggplot(trials, aes(zipf, log_rt, colour=factor(p_id))) +
  geom_point() +
  theme(legend.position="none")






(a) Trials coloured by Item







(b) Trials coloured by Participant

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.9 Parameter Retrieval
We can now fit a model that estimates the parameters we originally used to simulate the data. If you compare these estimates to the values we originally simulated, you can see that the estimates from our simulated sample are close to the ground truth.

m <- lmer(
  log_rt ~ 1 + zipf +
    (1 | i_id) +
    (1 | p_id),
  data = trials
)

summary(m)

Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
Formula: log_rt ~ 1 + zipf + (1 | i_id) + (1 | p_id)
   Data: trials

REML criterion at convergence: 1020.6

Scaled residuals: 
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.7332 -0.6655  0.0260  0.6379  2.9602 

Random effects:
 Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
 i_id     (Intercept) 0.008668 0.0931  
 p_id     (Intercept) 0.022208 0.1490  
 Residual             0.104945 0.3240  
Number of obs: 1500, groups:  i_id, 50; p_id, 30

Fixed effects:
             Estimate Std. Error t value
(Intercept)  6.792328   0.053743 126.384
zipf        -0.075392   0.009599  -7.854

Correlation of Fixed Effects:
     (Intr)
zipf -0.812

title: 2  LMMs: Random Intercepts
text:
2.10 lme4 Syntax
Finally, here is a quick breakdown of the lme4 syntax in the lmer() model:

log_rt ~ 1 + zipf — as in the linear model formula, this says that we want to model log_rt as a function of a fixed-effect intercept (1) and a slope that should be multiplied by zipf.
(1 | i_id) — this says that we want to model random intercepts for each unique value of i_id (the item IDs).
(1 | p_id) — this says that we want to model random intercepts for each unique value of p_id (the participant IDs).





DeBruine, Lisa M., and Dale J. Barr. 2021. “Understanding Mixed-Effects Models Through Data Simulation.” Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science 4 (1): 2515245920965119. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245920965119.


Locker, Lawrence, Lesa Hoffman, and James A. Bovaird. 2007. “On the Use of Multilevel Modeling as an Alternative to Items Analysis in Psycholinguistic Research.” Behavior Research Methods 39 (4): 723–30. https://doi.org/10.3758/BF03192962.


Raaijmakers, Jeroen G. W., Joseph M. C. Schrijnemakers, and Frans Gremmen. 1999. “How to Deal with ‘The Language-as-Fixed-Effect Fallacy’: Common Misconceptions and Alternative Solutions.” Journal of Memory and Language 41 (3): 416–26. https://doi.org/10.1006/jmla.1999.2650.
